content.text: What WebSockets Are

WebSocket is a full-duplex, bidirectional communication protocol over a single TCP connection. A client initiates the connection with a standard HTTP Upgrade handshake; once the server accepts, the connection is promoted from HTTP to the WebSocket protocol (ws:// or wss://). From that point, both sides can send messages to each other independently and at any time — there is no request-response cycle. The connection persists until one party sends a close frame or the underlying TCP connection drops.

WebSocket was standardised as RFC 6455 in 2011 and is now supported in every major browser, server-side runtime, and mobile platform. It was designed to replace hacks like HTTP long polling and Comet for real-time web applications, providing a proper full-duplex channel over the same port 80/443 infrastructure that HTTP uses.

attrs.alt: WebSocket lifecycle: HTTP Upgrade handshake → bidirectional WS frames → ping/pong keepalive → close handshake

content.text: Why WebSockets Matter for Agentic AI

Agentic AI applications often need rich, interactive communication patterns that SSE cannot support: the user can interrupt the model mid-generation, send clarifications, provide tool results interactively, or change the task direction in real time. WebSockets provide the bidirectional channel needed for these patterns without the overhead of opening new HTTP connections for each client-to-server message.

Multi-agent coordination also benefits from WebSockets: agents can exchange messages on a persistent channel, reducing the latency of the HTTP overhead that would otherwise accumulate across dozens of rapid inter-agent message exchanges.
Benefits
Full-duplex — both client and server can send messages at any time independently.
Low latency — no HTTP header overhead on each message after the initial handshake.
Binary and text frames — efficiently transmit structured data, audio, or binary blobs.
Built-in ping/pong frames for keepalive without application-level heartbeats.
Single connection serves both directions — no two-connection polling workarounds.
Works through most firewalls and proxies that pass port 80/443 traffic.
Trade-offs
Stateful connection — the server must track each open socket; horizontal scaling requires a shared pub/sub layer (Redis) or sticky sessions.
More complex than SSE for server-to-client-only use cases — adds unnecessary bidirectionality.
No automatic reconnection — client code must handle reconnect logic on disconnect.
Requires WebSocket-aware proxies and load balancers — some older proxies break WS upgrade.
Idle connections consume server file descriptors and memory even when no messages flow.
Frame-level backpressure must be implemented manually — TCP backpressure exists but is not surfaced cleanly.
How WebSockets Work

The WebSocket handshake starts as a plain HTTP GET request with special headers: Upgrade: websocket, Connection: Upgrade, and Sec-WebSocket-Key (a random base64-encoded nonce). The server validates the key, computes the accept value as base64(SHA1(key + magic_string)), and responds with HTTP 101 Switching Protocols. After that, the TCP stream is handed over to the WebSocket framing protocol.
WebSocket Frame Types
TEXT (0x1) — UTF-8 encoded text message. Used for JSON payloads.
BINARY (0x2) — arbitrary bytes. Used for audio, images, or protocol buffers.
PING (0x9) — keepalive probe; the recipient must reply with PONG.
PONG (0xA) — reply to PING. Confirms the connection is alive.
CLOSE (0x8) — initiates the close handshake. Carries a status code and optional reason.
CONTINUATION (0x0) — fragment of a multi-frame message.
Close Handshake

Closing a WebSocket cleanly requires a handshake: the initiating party sends a CLOSE frame, waits for the echo CLOSE frame from the other side, then closes the TCP connection. Status code 1000 signals a normal closure; 1001 means the server is going away (e.g., restarting); 1008 indicates a policy violation. Skipping the close handshake and abruptly closing the TCP connection causes the other side to report an error.
Architecture

Horizontally scalable WebSocket deployments use a pub/sub message broker (Redis, NATS) as the fan-out layer. Each server instance holds a set of open WebSocket connections. When a message needs to be broadcast to all clients (or a subset), it is published to a Redis channel; every server instance subscribed to that channel relays the message to its connected clients. This decouples the number of WebSocket connections from any single server instance.
When to Use WebSockets
Interactive chat interfaces where users can interrupt, clarify, or redirect the AI mid-response.
Collaborative tools — live document editing, shared whiteboards, multiplayer games.
Real-time data feeds — trading dashboards, live sports scores, sensor readings.
Voice and audio streaming — where binary frames carry audio chunks with minimal overhead.
Multi-agent coordination channels where agents exchange rapid messages.
Anything requiring client-to-server push without the overhead of new HTTP connections.
How to Implement: Python WebSocket Server
The websockets library provides a clean asyncio-based WebSocket server and client. Install with pip install websockets fastapi uvicorn.

# pip install websockets anthropic
import asyncio
import json
import websockets
from websockets.server import WebSocketServerProtocol
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

async def handle_connection(websocket: WebSocketServerProtocol):
    """Handle a single WebSocket connection."""
    print(f"Client connected: {websocket.remote_address}")
    conversation_history = []

    try:
        async for raw_message in websocket:
            try:
                message = json.loads(raw_message)
            except json.JSONDecodeError:
                await websocket.send(json.dumps({"error": "Invalid JSON"}))
                continue

            msg_type = message.get("type")

            if msg_type == "message":
                user_text = message.get("text", "")
                conversation_history.append({"role": "user", "content": user_text})

                # Notify client that generation is starting
                await websocket.send(json.dumps({"type": "start"}))

                # Stream the response back over the WebSocket
                full_response = ""
                with client.messages.stream(
                    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
                    max_tokens=1024,
                    messages=conversation_history,
                ) as stream:
                    for text_delta in stream.text_stream:
                        full_response += text_delta
                        await websocket.send(
                            json.dumps({"type": "token", "delta": text_delta})
                        )

                conversation_history.append(
                    {"role": "assistant", "content": full_response}
                )
                await websocket.send(json.dumps({"type": "done"}))

            elif msg_type == "reset":
                conversation_history.clear()
                await websocket.send(json.dumps({"type": "reset_ok"}))

    except websockets.exceptions.ConnectionClosedOK:
        print(f"Client disconnected cleanly: {websocket.remote_address}")
    except websockets.exceptions.ConnectionClosedError as e:
        print(f"Client disconnected with error: {e}")

async def main():
    async with websockets.serve(
        handle_connection,
        host="localhost",
        port=8765,
        ping_interval=30,   # send PING every 30 s
        ping_timeout=10,    # close if PONG not received in 10 s
    ) as server:
        print("WebSocket server running on ws://localhost:8765")
        await server.serve_forever()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
Python WebSocket Client

# pip install websockets
import asyncio
import json
import websockets

async def chat_client():
    """Interactive WebSocket chat client."""
    uri = "ws://localhost:8765"
    async with websockets.connect(
        uri,
        ping_interval=20,
        ping_timeout=10,
    ) as ws:
        print("Connected to AI chat. Type a message and press Enter.")
        print("Type 'quit' to exit, 'reset' to clear history.\n")

        while True:
            user_input = input("You: ").strip()
            if user_input.lower() == "quit":
                break
            if user_input.lower() == "reset":
                await ws.send(json.dumps({"type": "reset"}))
                resp = json.loads(await ws.recv())
                print(f"[{resp['type']}]\n")
                continue

            # Send user message
            await ws.send(json.dumps({"type": "message", "text": user_input}))

            # Collect streaming response
            print("AI: ", end="", flush=True)
            while True:
                raw = await ws.recv()
                msg = json.loads(raw)
                if msg["type"] == "start":
                    continue
                elif msg["type"] == "token":
                    print(msg["delta"], end="", flush=True)
                elif msg["type"] == "done":
                    print()  # newline
                    break
                elif msg["type"] == "error":
                    print(f"\n[error] {msg.get('error')}")
                    break

async def simple_ws_test():
    """One-shot test: send a message and print the streamed response."""
    async with websockets.connect("ws://localhost:8765") as ws:
        await ws.send(json.dumps({"type": "message", "text": "What is 2 + 2?"}))
        async for raw in ws:
            msg = json.loads(raw)
            if msg["type"] == "token":
                print(msg["delta"], end="", flush=True)
            elif msg["type"] == "done":
                print()
                break

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(chat_client())
FastAPI WebSocket Endpoint

FastAPI integrates WebSockets natively via the WebSocket class. This is the recommended approach for adding WebSocket support to an existing FastAPI application without running a separate websockets server.

# pip install fastapi uvicorn websockets anthropic
from fastapi import FastAPI, WebSocket, WebSocketDisconnect
from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

app = FastAPI()
client = Anthropic()

class ConnectionManager:
    """Track active WebSocket connections."""
    def __init__(self):
        self.active: list[WebSocket] = []

    async def connect(self, ws: WebSocket):
        await ws.accept()
        self.active.append(ws)

    def disconnect(self, ws: WebSocket):
        self.active.remove(ws)

    async def broadcast(self, message: str):
        for connection in self.active:
            await connection.send_text(message)

manager = ConnectionManager()

@app.websocket("/ws/chat")
async def websocket_chat(ws: WebSocket):
    await manager.connect(ws)
    conversation = []
    try:
        while True:
            raw = await ws.receive_text()
            try:
                message = json.loads(raw)
            except json.JSONDecodeError:
                await ws.send_text(json.dumps({"type": "error", "message": "Invalid JSON"}))
                continue

            if message.get("type") == "message":
                user_text = message.get("text", "")
                conversation.append({"role": "user", "content": user_text})

                await ws.send_text(json.dumps({"type": "start"}))

                full_text = ""
                with client.messages.stream(
                    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
                    max_tokens=1024,
                    messages=conversation,
                ) as stream:
                    for delta in stream.text_stream:
                        full_text += delta
                        await ws.send_text(json.dumps({"type": "token", "delta": delta}))

                conversation.append({"role": "assistant", "content": full_text})
                await ws.send_text(json.dumps({"type": "done"}))

    except WebSocketDisconnect:
        manager.disconnect(ws)
        print("Client disconnected")

# Run: uvicorn main:app --reload
# Connect: wscat -c ws://localhost:8000/ws/chat
# Or from browser: new WebSocket('ws://localhost:8000/ws/chat')
Real-World Example: Interactive AI Code Pair Programmer

An interactive coding assistant uses WebSockets so the developer can interrupt Claude mid-generation, ask for changes, or provide additional context without refreshing the page. When Claude starts generating a function and the developer types 'actually, make it async', the client sends a new message immediately. The server terminates the current generation, appends the clarification to the conversation, and starts a new stream. This kind of tight interaction loop is impossible with SSE (no client-to-server channel) and expensive with HTTP (a new connection per message).
Common Mistakes
Not handling WebSocketDisconnect — the server continues trying to write to a closed socket, raising exceptions.
Storing all connection state in local memory — fails on horizontal scale where different requests hit different servers.
Not implementing reconnect logic on the client — a network blip kills the connection with no recovery.
Using WebSockets when SSE is sufficient — adds complexity without benefit for pure server-to-client streaming.
Not validating WebSocket messages before processing — treat all incoming frames as untrusted input.
Missing ping/pong keepalive — idle connections behind NAT are closed by routers after 30–60 seconds of silence.
Best Practices
Send ping frames every 20–30 seconds and close connections that do not respond with pong.
Implement exponential backoff reconnect on the client: 1 s, 2 s, 4 s, 8 s, cap at 30 s.
Validate authentication on connection upgrade — check cookies or a token in the handshake URL.
Use a structured message envelope with a type field to multiplex different message kinds on one connection.
Use Redis pub/sub to fan messages across multiple server instances for horizontal scale.
Set connection limits per user to prevent resource exhaustion from clients that open many tabs.
Log connection open/close events with a unique connection ID for debugging latency issues.
Performance and Scalability

A single async Python server can maintain 10,000+ concurrent WebSocket connections because each connection is just a coroutine and a file descriptor with no blocked thread. The bottleneck is typically memory (per-connection buffers and state) and file descriptor limits. Raise the OS file descriptor limit (ulimit -n 65536) for high-concurrency deployments. For fan-out — sending the same message to many clients — use Redis pub/sub rather than iterating connections in Python, which is single-threaded for the GIL-protected dispatch loop.
Tooling and Ecosystem
websockets — Python asyncio WebSocket client and server library; the standard choice.
FastAPI — native WebSocket support via the WebSocket class and @app.websocket decorator.
Socket.IO — higher-level abstraction over WebSocket with rooms, namespaces, and automatic reconnect.
wscat — command-line WebSocket client for testing endpoints during development.
redis-py — asyncio-compatible Redis client for pub/sub fan-out across WebSocket server instances.
Nginx — WebSocket proxying with proxy_http_version 1.1; Upgrade and Connection headers required.

Security and Reliability

Always use wss:// (WebSocket over TLS) in production — ws:// transmits all frames in plaintext. Authenticate connections during the HTTP upgrade handshake: check a token in the URL query parameter or validate a session cookie in the Upgrade request headers. Validate every incoming message against a schema — a malicious client can send arbitrary frames. Implement a per-connection rate limit on incoming messages to prevent a single client from flooding the server with requests that trigger expensive LLM calls. Use a connection timeout for idle sockets (close after 30 minutes of inactivity) to reclaim file descriptors.
Mental Model

WebSocket is a telephone call. HTTP is like mailing letters — you write one, send it, wait for the reply, then write another. WebSocket opens a live phone line: both parties can speak at any time, interrupting or overlapping as needed, and the call stays open until one side hangs up. SSE is a one-way radio broadcast — you can hear the server, but you cannot talk back on the same channel. For interactive AI conversations where the user can interrupt, clarify, and redirect, a telephone (WebSocket) is the right tool; for watching the AI's output stream unidirectionally, a radio (SSE) is simpler and sufficient.
Key Takeaways
WebSocket provides full-duplex, bidirectional messaging over a single persistent TCP connection.
Use the websockets library for standalone async servers, or FastAPI's WebSocket class for integrated endpoints.
Implement ping/pong keepalive (every 20–30 s) to prevent idle connections from being killed by NAT.
Use Redis pub/sub for fan-out and horizontal scaling across multiple server instances.
Always use wss:// in production; validate auth during the handshake before accepting the connection.
Prefer SSE for pure server-to-client LLM streaming; choose WebSocket when the client needs to send messages mid-stream.
Implement client-side reconnect with exponential backoff to survive transient network failures.
